blocks.html: FACULTY HANDBOOK
Lewisville High School – Kill0ugh Campus
Fighting Farmers  ·  Est. 1897

This handbook contains information that will give you a better understanding of the policies, procedures, and daily operations of Lewisville High School- Killough. Throughout the year, you will receive additional information for your handbook. Please insert new information in the appropriate section as given. This is the abridged version of the information, not all items are included in this condensed faculty handbook.

1.  Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, as amended, 42 U.S.C. 2000d et seq., which prohibits discrimination on the basis of race, color, or national origin in programs and activities receiving Federal financial assistance.

2.  Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act of 1973, as amended, 29 U.S.C. 794, which prohibits discrimination on the basis of handicap in programs and activities receiving Federal financial assistance.

3.  Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972, as amended, 20 U.S.C. 1681 et seq., which prohibits discrimination on the basis of sex in education programs and activities receiving Federal financial assistance.
4.  The Age Discrimination Act of 1975, as amended, 42 U.S.C. 6101 et seq., which prohibits discrimination on the basis of age in programs or activities receiving Federal financial assistance.
5.  All regulations, guidelines, and standards lawfully adopted under the above statutes by the United States Department of Education.
Lewisville High School is the oldest of five high schools in the Lewisville Independent School District. It was opened in 1897. Killough Campus (9/10) was opened in 2005.
The school's colors are maroon and white and its mascot is "Big John,” the Fighting Farmer (a reference to Lewisville's early days as a rural community).
Stephanie Gore, Principal
Melanie Lowery, Assistant Principal
Ashlyn Straw, Assistant Principal
Clinton Marion, Assistant Principal
Tara Pierce, Lead Counselor
Daydrion Taylor
Miguel Perez
Sarah Moore- SAC
Kelly Casarez, Attendance Clerk
Kelly Bohon, Sub Coordinator

Classroom teachers will have planning periods for instructional preparation, parent teacher conferences, evaluation of student work, and planning. The schedule of planning periods is set at the campus level but must provide at least 450 minutes within each two-week period in blocks not less than 45 minutes within the instructional day. Teachers and librarians are entitled to a duty-free lunch period of at least 30 minutes.

Principals may amend employee times without lengthening the work day based on campus needs. Professional school personnel shall occasionally be required to work additional duty hours outside those stated above to complete those tasks associated with their positions included, but not limited to, campus staff meetings, student ARD meetings, open house, and other activities, events, or meetings.

Teachers are responsible for the furniture and equipment contained in their assigned room(s). Keys should be kept in possession of the teacher at all times. All lost keys must be reported in writing to Mrs.Straw within 24 hours of being lost/misplaced. In maintaining the classroom, teachers have the following responsibilities:

items: Expect that students will respect school property and hold those accountable that do not.
Frequently check equipment and furniture for writing or marks.
Safeguard all personal belongings in the classroom.
If you have surplus and or a need for additional furniture, please email Mrs.Straw, and she will guide you on the proper procedures for disposal or retrieval.
Keep your classroom free of clutter. Rooms should appear organized and clean.
If you have a problem in your classroom or in your area please let Mrs.Livingston know, and she will submit a work order to maintenance.

blocks.html: Custodial Services in LISD are contracted with ABM. Zulma Amaya is our lead Custodian. All custodial requests and or issues need to be emailed to Megan Livingston.
This includes:

items: Areas needing items restocked or cleaned
Ceiling tiles being changed out
Classroom light bulbs being replaced
Event set up- (Note: Your organization is responsible for assisting with setup and breakdown of your events.)

rows: SRO Officer Brown - 4504 - Next door to library

blocks.html: BUILDING PASSES:        No student is to be in the hall without his/her current teacher’s pass, or without an ID. Students should adhere to the 15/15 Rule which implies that no students should be leaving the classroom for any reason for the first and last 15 minutes of the period.  No more than one student at a time should be leaving the classroom.  The Accelerated Block has caused us to reemphasize the importance of instructional time.  Remember that students are better served when they spend time in the classroom receiving instruction.  Please minimize the amount of time students are out of class.

Instructions, information and various other memos will be forwarded to teachers through memorandums from the administration and various faculty members.  Please check your mailbox and e-mail frequently. SIGN UP FOR REMIND AND FOLLOW LHSK ON INSTAGRAM, FACEBOOK AND OUR SCHOOL WEB PAGE.

It will be necessary from time to time for teachers to cover classes during their planning periods.  Teachers will be compensated for covering classes during their conference period. If covering a class means that you will miss duty, then please notify admin as soon as possible.
Department chairpersons and subject area lead teachers may schedule meetings as often as they deem necessary. Monthly meetings are required.
DEPOSITS OF MONEY:  Deposits of any money you collect must be given to Kelly Bohon daily to deposit…MORE INFO NEEDED HERE ABOUT HOW AND WHEN AND RECEIPTS, ETC.

DO NOT DISMISS CLASS EARLY!  Please refrain from allowing students to congregate at doors until the dismissal bell rings. Do not release students early for lunch; wait for the second bell to ring so that cafeteria workers have an opportunity to prepare for the next lunch period.Throughout the year, we will host events or situations may arise that we will need to  dismiss students via the PA system. Please make sure to follow the bell schedule and be aware of any changes.

(HALL, CAFETERIA, RESTROOM, BUS, TARDY TABLE, ETC.) Teachers will be assigned morning/afternoon duty.  Teachers who have a third period conference may be assigned cafeteria duty.  If you will have to miss duty (absent, unexpected situation, etc.), it is your responsibility to find coverage for your duty spot.  All duty spots must be covered every day.
Please click the links below to access the LEAD handbook and Strive in Eduphoria.
LEAD HANDBOOK
EDUPHORIA/STRIVE

This area is available for teachers' use and enjoyment.  Be professional in its use.  Please remember to clean up after yourself!  Most necessary forms and materials for your use are located in the Faculty Center for your convenience.

Monthly faculty meetings are scheduled at 7:45am on the Wednesday following regularly scheduled Principal Learning Experience Meetings.These faculty meetings require mandatory attendance.  Other faculty meetings will be necessary, but they will be announced ahead of time to allow for teacher planning.  Faculty Meeting calendar invitations will be sent to all staff.

Faculty parking is located in the front/back of the school, and south end side of the school.  All faculty members are required to park in these designated areas unless the principal grants special permission for exception.  Faculty parking tags must be placed on all cars driven onto campus.  Failure to comply with the school's parking regulations could result in cars being towed away at owner expense.  Megan Livingston is responsible for faculty parking.

The designated restrooms on the first and second floors are reserved for faculty use only during the school day. Restrooms are located in the main office, the Fine Arts hallway, the downstairs workrooms, and the second-floor hallways.

All keys are the responsibility of Mrs. Straw.  We will continue to issue keys as we have in previous years.  Should you need additional keys or have special requests, you must see Mrs. Straw.  Please do not give your keys to students.  Please do not trade keys amongst staff members.
LISD EMPLOYEE HANDBOOK

The price for an adult lunch is $4.50. Student lunch is $3.40. Lunch schedules are from A - D.  Schedules cannot be altered for testing, labs, etc., without permission from the building principal.  In order for the lunch schedule to work effectively with four lunch periods, teachers must not let students leave the classroom early for a lunch period.  We do not offer lunch tutoring, students should not be in a teacher’s classroom during lunch.  For special requests, administration approval is needed.
All communications to teachers will be placed in your mailboxes in the Faculty Center or e-mailed to you. These boxes should be checked frequently.

There is a 5-minute passing period between classes for students to go from one classroom to another except between lunch when students have only 5 minutes.  It is important that teachers move to the doorways during these times to help supervise students in the hallways and to greet them as they enter the classroom.

NO room changes on a permanent or temporary nature are permitted without first securing the permission of the building principal.  Let the front office know of any changes made permanently or temporarily. If approved, please place a sign on your door.
All teachers are expected to attend and help supervise all extra-curricular activities which take place during the course of the normal day, such as pep rallies, special assemblies, etc.

The faculty and staff at LHS-K should dress professionally, in such a way as to distinguish our staff from our students. Inappropriate or questionable clothing will be addressed by administration. Staff members who do not meet professional dress expectations may be asked to change clothing. Photo ID Badges are a daily requirement of faculty dress code and are to be worn at all times.
Non Discretionary

Leave that is taken for personal or family illness, an emergency, or a death in the family is considered non-discretionary leave. This type of leave allows very little or no advance planning and will be granted to employees in the same manner as sick leave.
When it is necessary for a teacher to be absent without advance notice, he/she must do the following:
1.        Put your absence in Frontline Management.
2.        Provide lesson plans, and detailed instructions for the substitute teacher. These must be delivered to Kelly Bohon in the school office.
Discretionary

Leave that is taken at an employee’s discretion and that can be scheduled in advance is considered discretionary leave. An employee wishing to take discretionary personal leave must submit notice of the request in advance of the anticipated absence from his or her principal.

Discretionary personal leave will be granted on a first-come, first-served basis. The effect of the employee’s absence on the educational program or department operations, as well as the availability of substitutes, will be considered by the principal or supervisor.

No more than three (3) personal leave days may be taken consecutively without the prior written approval of the building principal.Per district policy, planned absences must receive pre-approval from Mrs. Gore.

Any teacher or staff member who knows about an absence in advance should fill out a Notice of Planned Absence Form and submit it to the Principal. These notices should be turned in at least 10 days in advance, if possible, to assure availability of a substitute teacher.  A Notice of Planned Absence must be submitted and approved whether or not a substitute will be required. Pre-arrangements by the teacher need to be made with Kelly Bohon prior to absence being submitted in the frontline.
School Business Absence

A Request for Planned Absence Form will need to be completed and have Mrs. Gore’s approval. Please submit no later than 10 days prior to absence. Email Kelly Bohon and the absence will be entered into the system for you after approval. Mrs. Bohon will follow up once approved and in the system.

Detailed lesson plans are an imperative part of the teaching process. Teachers should never go into the classroom unprepared. There must be goals, objectives, time lines, testing procedures, and teaching techniques to be used and incorporated into a lesson plan. Use of the lesson plan template is required.  Weekly lesson plans are required of all teachers and must be submitted by Monday at 8:20 am.

Conference/planning periods are designed to be built-in time for classroom teachers that allows for instructional preparation, parent and teacher conferences, and opportunities for evaluation of student work and planning. In compliance with the state required time of at least 45 minutes within each instructional day, our campus level policy expectations for LHSK teachers are the following:

items: Weekly PLC time - All core subject teams are expected to meet at least once a week for a minimum of 45 minutes. Non-core subject departments will need to establish a consistent schedule for PLC opportunities before or after school and submit the schedule to the presiding administrator. Administrators must be included on calendar invites for PLC meetings.

Weekly lesson planning - Lesson planning occurs weekly with course subject teams at least once a week for a minimum of 45 minutes. This time is separate from PLC meetings and should be utilized for preparation and team collaboration on lessons.

Flex Days - In an effort to minimize before and after school faculty meetings, there will be occasional flex day meetings that occur on designated Wednesdays throughout the school year. These meetings will typically require 30 minutes maximum of your 90-min conference period on those Wednesdays.

blocks.html: When leaving campus during contract hours, you are required to text or email your LEAD appraiser.  Please text or email when you have returned to campus.

SUBSTITUTES - LESSON PLANS:  Lesson plans, class rolls, and specific instructions must be turned in for substitute teacher use.  These are due by 7:45 a.m. on the day a substitute teacher is required.  Problems with substitute teachers should be reported to Kelly Bohon immediately.  Please remember that substitutes will have no computer access.  You are required to have a minimum of 3 days of emergency sub plans prepared at all times.
Substitute Folder

● The teacher will have available on his/her desk a Substitute Folder with pertinent information for the substitute. It is important that the substitute be able to locate this folder immediately so please have it on your desk with a note with clear instructions for the day.
● Please make sure that co-teacher and/or Department chairs are aware of your plans in case of questions.
● Inside the substitute folder will be materials of importance such as:
✔Current Class Roster attendance sheets (multiple copies left in sub folder, must have student name and id, one class section per page)
✔ Class Schedule
✔ Lesson Plans, syllabus
✔ Emergency lesson plans
✔ Emergency procedures including evacuation route (yellow folder must be hanging next to exit door with copy of current roster)
✔ Instructions regarding medication and grading (check every 9 weeks to keep current)
✔ Important or unusual information about students
✔ Specific instructions to the substitute
✔ List of supervisory responsibilities such as duty schedule including map highlighting duty area
Canvas sites are vital tools to engage students, enhance collaboration, encourage parental involvement, and extend learning beyond the classroom.

ALL TEACHERS NEED TO ESTABLISH A CANVAS SITE FOR THEIR CLASSES/SUBJECTS. Do not require passwords or enrollment keys for students/parents. Each site needs a format/opportunity for students and parents to have two-way communication with the teacher through the website.

Assistant Principal, Clinton Marion, is in charge of textbooks.  Teachers are responsible for the accounting of all textbooks issued to them. Periodic textbook checks are recommended. See handout on textbook procedures.

Staff are required to be at school by 8:00 a.m. and in their classrooms by 8:45 a.m. They must remain on campus until 4:30 p.m. Any exceptions to these times must be cleared with the building principal.
Smoking is not permitted at any time in the school building or on school grounds.
Weekly Tutoring

Teachers are required to provide 90 minutes of tutorials every week. Tutorial times should be offered in the morning AND the afternoon. Teachers will publicly post their tutoring dates/times. Teachers should have any student attending tutoring sign the campus tutoring log via QR code for documentation purposes.
Mandatory Tutoring

Teachers should assign mandatory tutoring to all students who are failing at the end of the 3rd, 6th, and 8th weeks of each grading period. Triplicate Mandatory Tutoring forms are available in the Faculty Center.

If a student fails to attend a scheduled mandatory tutoring session, the teacher should submit an office referral and attach a copy of the Mandatory Tutoring Form to the student's Assistant Principal's door.

Daily announcements are made during ADVISORY. Please submit all announcements through the Google Form announcement request with Clinton Marion/Megan Livingston no later than 8:30 am the day before. ANNOUNCEMENT FORM

The BLT is mandated by state law to serve in an advisory capacity to the administrators. Members will include faculty members (departmental representation), parents and community members. All departments are required to have 1 representative serve on the BLT.

Mrs. Straw, assistant principal, is responsible for the maintenance of all school facilities.  If you have the need for maintenance or repair services, please complete a work order request by emailing Megan Livingston.  Mrs. Straw and Mrs. Livingston will determine whether the maintenance staff on our campus can complete the work or if the request will need to be referred to the central maintenance department.  The goal of the maintenance staff is to provide a quality response to all of your requests with the safety of our students and staff as our highest priority.

All staff members are required by law to promptly report suspected bullying or harassment. Reports may be made to any campus administrator or other personnel as indicated in LISD Board Policy FFI(LOCAL).pdf and FFH(LOCAL).pdf.

All school activities are to be entered on the school calendar.  Please give pertinent information to Megan Livingston in the main office no later than 2 weeks prior to the activity. This includes all booster club activities as well. Our campus calendar can be found in OUTLOOK: LHSK Staff Calendar. If you need assistance adding it in your Outlook, please get with Mrs.Livingston.

Report any suspected cases of child abuse to a principal and counselor. It is a state law that all suspected cases are reported, Failure to report child abuse within 48 hours is a violation of state law.  ALL CPS Case Numbers are REQUIRED to be emailed to the principal and student’s counselor. https://www.dfps.texas.gov/child_protection//

The atmosphere a teacher establishes in the classroom is very important in developing a good teaching-learning environment. Take pride in your classroom.  Use decor related to your subject area. Maintain proper lighting, etc. No personal furniture, coffee pots, fridges, microwaves, etc. may be used in the classroom setting. Let students know what their responsibilities are in helping you maintain a clean and orderly classroom.

Teachers are responsible for their classrooms and for all furniture and equipment contained within them. Classroom keys issued by the office should remain in the teacher’s possession at all times. In maintaining proper care of classrooms, teachers have the following responsibilities:

items: Classroom doors must be locked and secured whenever students are in the classroom.

Require students to take proper care of desks, tables, and other classroom furniture. Desks, furniture, and equipment should be inspected regularly for unnecessary markings or damage. Any student who willfully damages classroom furniture or equipment should be referred to the principal or assistant principal. Students are not permitted to sit on top of student or teacher desks.
At the end of each school day, turn off lights, leave chairs and desks in reasonably good order, secure your computer, and lock the classroom door.
Safeguard personal belongings and money during the school day, and avoid leaving valuables in the classroom after school hours.
After using audio-visual equipment that is not normally kept in the classroom, return it to its designated location at the end of the class period in which it was used.
For energy efficiency, turn off lights whenever the room is not in use.
Window coverings on classroom doors must remain open at all times unless otherwise directed by administration or during a LOCKDOWN.

blocks.html: A staff member must be in the classroom supervising the students at all times. Only in case of an emergency should a teacher be late to class or leave a class unattended. If such an emergency should occur, teachers must do the following:

items: Make reasonable efforts to see that the students are supervised.
Contact a teacher next door and ask him/her to check on your students.
Send word to the office that you must be out of the classroom.
Give specific instructions to your students.
Notify the office when you have returned to supervise the class.

blocks.html: Failure to do any of the above could place a teacher in a position of being charged with
negligence should an incident occur in the classroom while it is unsupervised. This will
be documented and could affect the teacher’s annual appraisal.

By board policy, Lewisville High School Killough is a closed campus. This means that students are not permitted to leave campus at any time between 8:50 a.m. and 4:15 p.m. without permission from a school administrator and a parent or guardian. Teachers must never send students off campus for any reason without going through the building principal first.

All club and organization activities must be held before school, after school, or in the evening. Mrs. Lowery oversees all student-related school activities. All scheduled activities must be submitted to Megan Livingston and included on the school calendar. Student groups (clubs) offered on campus must be student-initiated and student-led, with a staff “monitor” present at all meetings. All students under the age of 18 who participate in these groups must have a signed parent permission form on file.
Please refer to the LISD Non-Sponsored/Non-Curriculum-Related Student Group Start-Up Process outlined below:

items: Student(s) requests non-school sponsored group (club) form from front office with Ms. Werstak
Student(s) submit page 2 to Mrs. Gore: if approved, Principal will sign and have form returned to student(s)

blocks.html: EMPLOYEE COMPLAINTS and GRIEVANCES
Please remember to keep your conversations professional and respectful of student privacy both on and off campus. We are never off duty as representatives of Lewisville High School.

All staff members are responsible for compliance with federal and state law, as well as district policy on the use of intellectual property and copyrighted materials. Please see the following LISD Board policies:
CY(LOCAL)
CY(LEGAL)

Assistant Principal, Ashlyn Straw is in charge of custodial needs. In most cases, simple janitorial requests can be routed to Zulma.  If, however, you discover that your needs are not being met, please notify Mrs. Straw at once.
Deposits of any money you collect must be given to Kelly Bohon daily to  deposit…MORE INFO NEEDED HERE ABOUT HOW AND WHEN AND RECEIPTS, ETC.

All fundraising activities must be approved by Mrs. Gore. Please submit the Fundraising Activity Approval Form, available from Mrs. Bohon, prior to making any commitments related to fundraising. Do not confer with representatives unless they have been cleared through Mrs. Gore’s office.

Teachers shall preview all movies, clips or videos including short clips or segments in venues such as You-Tube or PBS before using them in a classroom. Teachers must then obtain approval of all films and videos from a campus principal or designee, such as an assistant principal or department chair. The District expects that only clips or segments will be used to emphasize or illustrate subject content; however, in the rare instance when a movie might need to be shown in its entirety, regardless of rating or grade level, the campus principal must approve.
R-Rated Movies Or Clips

Under no circumstances at elementary, middle or high school should a film that is R-rated be shown. For high schools only, the campus principal may approve segmented clips if pre-viewed and proposed by a teacher; otherwise, R-rated movies will not be shown. At the high school level, a film or video that is rated “PG-13” and that is related to the instructional program may be shown only with prior written permission from the parents.
Pg-13 Rated Movies Or Clips
For middle school, if related to the instruction, a film or video that is rated “PG-13” may be shown only with prior written permission from the parents.

At the elementary school level, a film or video that is rated “PG” and that is related to the instructional program may be shown in its entirety only with prior written permission from the parents as well as the building principal. Short clips or segments of “PG” films used for instructional purposes do not have to be pre-approved.
G Rated Movies or Clips
A G rated movie or clip will only need principal approval if planning to show the entire movie.
The State of Texas requires all students to be tested on their levels of physical fitness.
Students are to be tested once a year, and the tests will be performed at various times
throughout the school year.
LISD EMPLOYEE HANDBOOK

Each teacher has a duty to exemplify the respect we have for education, our students, each other and our campus. To support this expectation, staff are asked to ensure that their personal appearance, conduct, and classroom environment reflect professionalism.
***Staff should NEVER text or direct message an individual student unless an
administrator or parent is included in the communication.***
Teachers planning to attend conventions, conferences, etc., should talk with their department head and LEAD appraiser prior to making plans. Final approval will need to come from the principal.
Gradebook Policies
PROGRESS REPORTS/REPORT CARDS:
UIL CALENDAR

The public address system will be used for announcements at designated times so as not to conflict on a regular basis with instructional time. Unless there is a need, classes will not be interrupted by the public address system or telephones.
All purchases for instructional supplies, materials, etc., must be done through the department head. Do not purchase anything without a P.O. and approval per Kelly Bohon.

The sponsor of an organization is the school's representative.  Since these organizations are a part of the school program, the faculty representative will be expected to see that the activities are in keeping with public school philosophy.  The sponsor's presence at all regular meetings is very essential.  All businesses of the organization should be approved by the principal before action takes place.  Any funds belonging to the specific clubs or groups will be kept in the Lewisville High School Activity fund and all fiscal and budgeting procedures should be followed exactly.

Teachers and staff are encouraged to utilize social media when appropriate as a means for celebrating classroom successes and for general information and communication. Please use professionalism and responsibility with social media. SUBMIT SOCIAL MEDIA POSTS TO STEPHANIE HALL.
See Student Handbook

Teachers are expected to enforce student ID procedures. Each student is required to wear his/her ID on a lanyard at all times while in the building. Students who fail to follow this requirement will receive a referral and will be subject to consequences according to the frequency of the offense:

Students are required to wear their school ID on a lanyard to enter the school and are to keep it on at all times in the building. Students who fail to follow this requirement will be required to get an ID Sticker ($1) and or purchase a new ID for $5.

Students may not wear another student’s ID at any time. Staff members should confiscate misused ID’s and report the student to the appropriate administrator for disciplinary action. ID infractions do not reset at the semester.
Per Texas House Bill 1481, all schools are required to prohibit students from using or displaying personal communication devices (phones, smart watches, etc.) during the instructional school day.

Access to the District’s electronic communications system is a privilege, not a right.  All users shall be required   to acknowledge receipt and understanding of administrative regulations governing use of the system and shall agree in writing to allow monitoring of their use and compliance with such regulations and guidelines.  Noncompliance may result in suspension of access or termination of privileges and other disciplinary action consistent with District policies. Violations of law may result in criminal prosecution as well as disciplinary action by the District.

Electronic mail transmissions and other use of the electronic communications system by students and employees are not private and may be monitored at any time by designated District staff to ensure appropriate use.

Staff must follow ALL LISD Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) guidelines. Two points of emphasis—DO NOT SHARE PASSWORDS WITH OTHERS, and monitor student use of all computers. Various deadlines for completion and return of computer forms will be announced throughout the year. In order for the computer service to be effective, such deadlines must be met by all staff. Under no circumstances should a student be using a teacher’s computer.

All staff members are responsible for compliance with federal and state law, as well as district policy on the use of intellectual property and copyrighted materials. Please see the following LISD Board policies:
CY(LOCAL)
CY(LEGAL)

All teacher trips, other than regularly scheduled athletic events, must be entered on the calendar with Megan Livingston. A Request for School Trips, Conventions, and Workshops form must be completed and submitted to the building principal for approval, along with any transportation requests. For trip request forms, see Kelly Bohon.
All staff members are expected to volunteer for one activity in the fall and one activity in the spring.  Please check emails for faculty assistance.

sections.title: ELIGIBILITY FOR STUDENTS/STATE LAW FOR UIL

blocks.html: Chapter 33, Senate Bill I states that a student must pass all courses he/she is taking to be eligible to participate in any school related extra-curricular activity and/or co-curricular activity where admission is charged.  This rule is in effect at the beginning of the second six-weeks of a new school year.  Students who fail courses at the end of a six-week grading period will be evaluated again at the three-week period following the end of the six-weeks.  If these students are passing all classes at this time, they become eligible seven days after the evaluation date.  It is most important that teachers remember that once they post six-week grades or a three-week evaluation grade, they cannot change these grades for eligibility purposes.  If there is any doubt about students' final six-weeks or three-week grades, and the students are in activities where eligibility is involved, teachers should give the students incomplete scores.  For eligibility purposes, an "I" is an F until it becomes an official grade.  Please refer to the principal's handout for specifics on the new eligibility law.  PLEASE SEE UIL CALENDAR SENT AT THE BEGINNING OF THE YEAR.
LISD Secondary Grading Guidelines
Follow all instructions that are provided during the school year. For information regarding gradebook contact Erika Cowan.
● Every assignment in the gradebook system should be dated and identified appropriately so it can be accurately interpreted by others
● Gradebooks and grade records are considered part of the permanent record system, are the property of the school, and are subject to inspection at any time
● Only teachers or designated school staff should handle or have access to any form of grading records, including student progress reports
● All grade records are confidential, and no student should have access to grade records or the gradebook system at any time.
Gradebook Submit Dates

Remember, only 9 week grades determine eligibility EXCEPT for the 1st 6 weeks of the school year. UIL eligibility may be lost after the 1st 6 weeks and every 9 week grading period thereafter. The exam and semester grades are not used to determine eligibility.
Semester grades determine credit awarded. Students do have the opportunity to regain eligibility every 3 week progress reporting times.
Stephanie Castro
Tests given
EOC/STAAR- English 1. English II,Biology an AlgebraI PSAT, ACT, AP, SAT, TSI. A testing calendar will be given once it is finalized for the TRAID.

All copy machines are located in the workrooms and  the Faculty Center.   If a copy machine or any other equipment stops operating correctly, contact Mrs. Werstak immediately.  If Mrs.Werstak is unavailable, please leave to see Mrs.Dunn.  Students are NOT to be sent to use the copy machines.
Please see:  https://www.lisd.net/our-district/all-departments/counseling-services/about-us
LISD Policy - See Section II of this handbook.  All videos shown in the classroom must have prior approval from the building principal or his designee.
LEWISVILLE HIGH SCHOOL
LIBRARY POLICIES AND PROCEDURES
(August, 1995)

The Lewisville High School Library is dedicated to serving the staff and students in an efficient and thorough manner.  The following guidelines will facilitate effective use of library facilities and materials:
1.        Use of Printed Materials
All materials in the library may be checked out for an extended time by any staff member.  The staff is asked to check out materials in person rather than sending a student for the items.

Teachers needing special materials are encouraged to discuss their needs with the librarians.  The library staff welcomes all teacher requests for materials and will do its best to acquire these materials. Forms for ordering books are available from the librarian.  The librarian is also available to select materials in specific subjects suggested by the classroom teacher.
Teachers may put special materials on reserve in the library so that their students may come in to use the items.  Students will not be allowed to check out reserve materials.

A wide variety of professional materials is located in the Professional Library, including guides to free and inexpensive materials for use in the classroom.  Notices of newly acquired professional materials will be posted from time to time.
2.        Library Hours
The library will be open Mondays and Thursdays from 7:30 a.m. until 5:00 p.m., and Fridays from 7:30 a.m. until 4:00 p.m.
3.        Special Use of the Library

Requests for special use of the library facilities are to be made through the principal's office.  Groups or individuals using the facility for presentations or meetings are responsible for returning all items to their original location and for the general condition of the facility.  After receiving the principal's approval for using the library, the group or individual should contact the librarians for specific requirements.
4.        Use of Non-Print Materials

All audio-visual software materials will be housed in and ordered through the library.  T.E.A. guidelines mandate that all non-print materials be processed and catalogued through the library.  The card catalogue will contain information and the location of individual items.
5.        Using the Library During School Hours

The library is available for class activities at all times.  The classroom teacher may reserve the facility by signing up in the library in advance.  A maximum of two classes may be accommodated during a single class period.  The classroom teacher is encouraged to sign up well in advance.  When signing up for a class, a teacher may make special requests for books to be pulled, bibliographies to be prepared, films or other audio-visual items to be shown or for special tours or orientations to the library and its materials.  Consultation with the library staff is encouraged for special requests.
Classroom teachers are expected to remain with their classes for guidance and assistance at all times.

Any teacher sending a student to the library for independent study must write a pass for that student. The pass should give the student's name, teacher's name, date, time the student left the classroom, and the nature of the student's work.  Students will be expected to remain in the library until the end of the period unless the teacher gives a specific return time.  All passes will be signed by the librarian and returned to the teacher's mailbox indicating that the student remained in the library until the end of the class period.  The teacher should indicate in writing if the student is to return before the end of the period.  Teachers are asked not to send more than five students to the library unless special arrangements have been made in advance.
The library is open before school, after school, and during lunch to all students.  Passes are not required during this time.
6.        Audio-Visual Equipment

Teachers needing audio visual equipment are requested to reserve that equipment in advance through the library.  A daily sign up sheet is available for reserving equipment.  Sign up sheets are provided in nine-week blocks.  Teachers should sign up for each day they plan to use the equipment.  Teachers are responsible for picking up and returning their own equipment.  Students are not to transport the VCR/TV units.  This is district policy.  Students, with a note from the teacher, may transport other equipment.  At the end of the day, equipment must be returned except for those checked out for long-term use.  TV's and VCR's will be returned every day.  NO VCR/TV units will be transported to the portables.

Teachers are asked not to loan equipment to other teachers unless arrangements have been made through the library.  An orientation on equipment use will be provided for the staff at the beginning of the school year.  Anyone having questions concerning equipment use and operation is encouraged to discuss them with the librarian.  A few tips on effective operation of the equipment:

a.        Never move a projector or piece of equipment until it has cooled completely.  Allow the fan to run until completely cool.  This prolongs the life of the bulb, some of which cost over $20 each. Carefully handled, a bulb can last for many hours.  If a bulb burns out, send a student to the library with a note specifying the bulb type, which is found on the pocket on the piece of equipment.  The teacher can also wait until the bulb is cool, remove it, and exchange it for a new one.  When inserting a bulb, never touch it with the fingers.  Use the foam insert inside the box or use a tissue.  Touching the bulb shortens its life greatly.  If a teacher encounters other problems with equipment, the librarian will be happy to come to the classroom to assist the teacher.

b.        If a piece of equipment breaks down or becomes damaged, please let the library staff know as soon as possible.  In this way, equipment can be repaired and maintained in good working order at all times.
c.        The library staff would like to discourage the operation of equipment by students.
7.        Region XI Service Center

A variety of audiovisual software items may be ordered through the Region XI Service Center located in Fort Worth.  Catalogues and order cards are available through the library.  The service center also offers many other services such as in-service programs, computer searches, duplication of tapes and films, laminating and other technical processing.  The center has the capability of transferring 16mm films to videotapes.  Handouts on their services will be distributed periodically.

Materials are delivered and picked up Tuesday mornings.  Teachers will be notified when their requested materials arrive.  Teachers receiving the material are responsible for returning it to the library on time. Please be prompt in returning items so that others may use them.

Teachers are encouraged to order materials early to be assured of receiving them at the most desirable time.  When ordering films, the last four numbers of the teacher's social security number are the teacher number and the number for LHS is 007.
8.        In-service Programs
The library staff looks forward to providing in-service programs for teachers during the school year. Anyone having ideas or requests for such programs is invited to discuss them with the librarians.
9.        Production

The library staff is available to assist teachers in the production of classroom materials.  Such production includes videotaping of television programs and of classroom projects or activities, making transparencies, and producing sound slide presentations.  Teachers wishing to videotape television programs may sign up in the library and should use tapes provided by their department.  Materials are laminated every Tuesday morning.
10.        Budget

During the early part of the second semester, the librarians will begin to formulate the budget for the coming year.  Included in the budget are all items of audiovisual software to use in the classroom. These items include all professionally prepared kits, blank tapes of all kinds, and other consumable or non-consumable pieces of software.  The librarian will work closely with the department chairpersons and individual teachers in formulating budget requests.  The librarian is also responsible for ordering, processing, and dissemination of these materials.

The library staff would like to see many more teachers making use of the library.  With over 25,000 volumes and audio visual software, the library offers a wealth of information for research and many excellent books for reading pleasure.  The library staff welcomes the opportunity to serve the students and teachers.  If the library does not have what you need, please let the librarians know.  One of the goals of the library is to offer resources and services that enhance the teachers' abilities to provide good lessons and teaching to all students.
Lewisville ISD

DATE ISSUED:  08/28/95
LDU-35-95                                ADOPTED
EFE (LOCAL)-X
Questions and Answers Regarding Showing Movies/Videos

As DVD/VHS loans start to increase I wanted to take a moment and remind all faculty and staff of some basic and general copyright information. This is by no means all inclusive but gives you a little overview with slight humor.
Can I show a movie to my class as a reward?
Short Answer:  No.
Fine Print:  Sorry.  No exceptions.
Can I or a substitute teacher show a movie to my class as part of a lesson I am teaching?
Short Answer:  Maybe.

Fine Print:  Movies/Films (VHS or DVD) may be shown as part of the classroom as long as ALL of the following are true:  a) Lewisville High School owns a copy of the film that was legally obtained b) the film relates to the LISD curriculum being taught AT THE TIME the video is shown and c) showing the film is accompanied by direct instruction -- in other words, students are doing something as they watch the film that demonstrates their mastery of the
LISD Curriculum being addressed through its viewing.
Can I show a rated R movie to my class?
Short Answer:  No.

Fine Print:  According to LISD policy and the LHS Handbook at the elementary school level, a film or video that is rated “PG” and that is related to the instructional program may be shown only with prior written permission from the parents. At the middle school level, a film or video that is rated “PG-13” and that is related to the instructional program may be shown only with prior written permission from the parents. Under no circumstances, at any instructional level, shall a film or video that is rated “R” be shown. Films and videos that have no rating may be shown only after being reviewed and approved by the campus principal.
Can I show my class an episode of a television show that I recorded with my VCR/DVR/DVD?
Short Answer:  Again, yes.  But there are some rules.

Fine Print:  As with movies, television programming may only be rebroadcast in the classroom for educational purposes.  (In other words, you may not allow your students to watch the NCAA tournament, or any other TV program, solely as a reward.)  Additionally, the showing of material that has been recorded from television must also be accompanied by direct instruction and the broadcast must occur at the time that students are learning the material that the program relates to.  However, and this is also important, material that is recorded from television for the purpose of rebroadcast in the classroom must be shown within 10 days of the program's original air date.  That means that if a great program on genetics, for example, airs on PBS in October, but your class is not studying genetics until March, copyright law indicates that you may NOT record it and hang on to it until you are studying that topic.  This also means that if a great show about genetics, for example, airs on PBS in October, AND your class IS studying genetics the very week that it is aired, you MAY record it and show it to your class, but you are NOT allowed to show it next year (or next month) unless it just happens to be aired again. Sorry.
Can I, or my students, use multimedia clips (like music, film or digital images) in presentations that we create for the classroom.
Short Answer:  Yes.  But there are some rules.

Fine Print:   Generally, up to 10% (or 3 minutes for film/30 seconds for music -- whichever is shorter) may be used in a presentation unless the copyright holder specifically prohibits it, but ONLY if credit is given to the copyright owner.  In regards to digital images, no more than 3 images by a single artist may be used in a multimedia presentation without permission of the artist and again, credit must be given to the copyright holder.  However, and this is important, these excerpts, segments and/or images may NOT be (re)posted to the internet without specific written permission of the original copyright holder.  In other words, you may show the presentation in class, but you may not post it on your webpage until you have received permission for the copyright holders of any/all multimedia that you have used in the presentation.
Can I make a copy of printed material and distribute to my class?
Short Answer: Maybe :)

Fine Print: First off, items that were published prior to 1923 are in what is known as the "public domain."  Items in the public domain are not "owned" by anyone in particular and can be used without acquiring permission.

That said, if the material you wish to use is not part of the public domain, the following rules apply:  Teachers may make enough copies for each student to have one copy of the following items:  poem less than 250 words, excerpts of 250 words from a poem greater than 250 words, articles, stories, or essays less than 2,500 words, excerpt from a longer work (10% of work or 1,000 words, whichever is less--but a minimum of 500 words), one chart, picture, diagram, graph, cartoon or picture per book or per periodical issue, and two pages (max) from an illustrated work less than 2,500 words (like a children's books).  In all of these instances, teachers are strongly encouraged to obtain permission from the copyright holder but the law states that teachers may forego obtaining permission in instances wherein there is not enough time to do so.

Teachers may make a single copy of the entire work of the following items, if it is going to be used only for the teacher's own instructional use, and will NOT be distributed to students: a complete chapter from a book, an article from a periodical, a short story, short essay, or short poem a chart, graph, diagram, drawing , cartoon, picture from a book, periodical or newspaper.
Is this everything I need to know about copyright in the classroom?
Short Answer:  Probably not.

Fine Print:  Below are some additional resources that you may find helpful as you attempt to provide your students with the most meaningful and effective educational experience possible while also adhering to the letter of the (copyright) law.

Additionally, if you have any other questions, just email me.  I may not know the answer, but I'll find someone who does.  And when I do, I'll post the answer here so that we can all benefit from your inquisitiveness.

Finally, the key to all of this is that, whenever possible, you should seek permission of the copyright holder when using material that was not created specifically for use in your classroom.  Recently, several of our teachers have written to various companies (HBO and Warner Bros. to name just a couple) in order to obtain permission to show their students something which these corporations owned.  In each instance that I've been told about, the teachers heard back immediately and were not only given permission to use the material but in one case, was even given curriculum materials to use in order to provide the "direct instruction" that is such an important part of "fair use."  The moral of the story??  When in doubt, ask.
Did you become a librarian just so that you could keep teachers from showing movies in class?
Short Answer:  Nah.  It's just an added bonus.  :)

Fine Print:  I abhor this stuff as much as you guys do... but I feel I would be remiss if I didn't at least tell you what the law states.  Ignorance may be bliss, but not when you find your name at the top of a subpoena.
Brandi Rosales, M.L.S
Library Media Specialist
Lewisville High School
Facebook  Twitter

1.        BE POSITIVE ABOUT MAKING A NEW FRESH START, be it moving to a new town, a new school or a new classroom.  Sometimes you may have all of these changes, including a change in grades as well, and it may seem terribly overwhelming.  Allow time and patience to do their work as you are doing the best you can.
2.        GIVE SUPPORT TO YOUR COLLEAGUES.  New teachers will need to feel welcomed by those of us who have been here longer.

3.        MAINTAIN A REALISTIC PERSPECTIVE.  Avoid taking a student's misbehavior as a personal attack on you.  Children usually misbehave for different reasons, most of which does not have anything to do with how the child feels about his or her teacher.

4.        YOUR FEELINGS ARE OK.  Allow yourself to be frustrated, irritated, or even angry sometimes.  You are a role model for your students, and it is OK for them to know that you are human.  Avoid letting these feelings stay with you for a long time.  Deal with them as soon as you are able.

5.        LET GO OF HARD FEELINGS.  Avoid retaining hard feelings about students, parents or colleagues that you have had from the previous year.   You will free yourself of the "burden" of the emotional strain that is created by "holding on" to angry feelings.

6.        GIVE PRAISE.  Avoid negative remarks about your students however they may be directed (about them or other adults).  Look for the positives (everybody has some good points).  Let your students, colleagues, administrators and parents know what you like about them.  You will feel rewarded inside.
7.        CLARIFY YOUR EXPECTATIONS - for yourself, your students, the parents and from the people you work with.

8.        LEARN TO MANAGE YOUR TIME IN ORDER TO BE AN EFFECTIVE TEACHER.  You need to be good to yourself.  Learn to take time for yourself, to relax, do sports or do something for the pleasure of it.  You will feel refreshed in the classroom, and your students will benefit too.

9.        ACCEPT THAT NOT EVERYONE WILL LIKE YOU.  Be yourself - it is only human not to be liked by everyone.  Developing friendships with colleagues may add to your day at the work setting but is not a must for constructive working relationships.

10.        MAINTAIN A SENSE OF HUMOR.  Try to keep a balance by having some humor along with the serious business of the workday.  You, of course, will need to be firm in setting the limits in order for the necessary tasks to be done.
FINALLY, REMIND YOURSELF THAT YOU ARE A GOOD TEACHER AND YOU ARE TRYING TO DO YOUR BEST!
BEST WISHES FOR A GOOD BEGINNING.
When possible Admin will use the PA System to alert staff of an Emergency situation. Please listen carefully and adhere to the directions given.

In case of a tornado alert, please lead your students to the designated areas and have them sit on the floor with their knees pulled up to their chests.  They will be grouped by class and must stay together.  A passageway must be left in the middle of the hallway for others who must pass through.  Keep the students sitting closely together from the wall out.  All classroom  doors to the hall should be closed.  Maintain order during the extent of the alert.  Allow no standing, yelling, or disorderly conduct.  Report to the office all incidents of misconduct.  Give orders clearly and calmly and require them to be followed.  Maps for designated areas should be posted in each classroom by the doorway.

The fire signal is an alarm with strobe lights.  Leave all personal belongings and where they are and walk quickly to the exit designated for your classroom.  Each room should have an emergency exit guide posted.
SECURE
LOCKDOWN

A student must use the QR code to schedule an appointment with the nurse. The health room will be open during the school day from 8:50 a.m. until 4:00 p.m.  Please do not send students before or after these hours unless it is an emergency.  Tylenol, aspirin, etc., are not kept in the health room.  Students are allowed to carry and administer their own medication (except for controlled substances/prescriptions) with the proper paperwork (any student with over-the-counter medication will have a nurse form with them at all times when on campus). See Nurse for forms.  All medications should be reported to the school nurse so that it can be recorded on the student's health record.

All visitors to LHSK are required to check in with the office upon arrival.  They will receive a “Visitor” badge at that time.  If you see people in the building without a “Visitor” badge, please ask them if you can be of assistance.  After they state the nature of their business, please escort them to the main office. Please call the office or notify an administrator immediately if you feel uneasy or suspicious.
Students are not allowed to bring visitors to class with them.  Parents are allowed to visit classrooms only with agreement by the principal and the teachers.

By Texas law, a student must be in attendance in classes a minimum of 90% of the time the classes are taught.  Failure to be in attendance the required 90% could result in loss of credit in any class where excessive absences occur.  The 90% requirement is inclusive of absences identified as excused, unexcused, or for extra-curricular activities that exceed the 10 allowed.

THE ATTENDANCE OFFICE RECORD WILL BE THE OFFICIAL SCHOOL RECORD: The attendance office records will serve as the official student attendance record.  Individual teacher grade books can be used (and often are) for legal purposes.  It is imperative that they be correct.

Policy requires that the reason for a student's absence be stated in writing and signed by a parent or guardian of the student.  The attendance office will accept phone calls from a parent or guardian as being the written statement and will document these calls on student records.

A uniform coding system is used for marking student attendance.  This coding system is E - Excused Absences, U  - Unexcused Absences, P  – Change to present with documentation, and T  - Tardy.  Codes are automated when a teacher takes attendance. Attendance staff can update codes.
Second? period is the official ADA accounting period. Attendance must be submitted by the teacher within 10 minutes on either side of the designated funding time.
Attendance must be submitted by the teacher before the end of the period. Ideally, attendance will be submitted before the students who take shuttles to other campuses leave the classroom.

Make-up work or tests are permitted for all excused absences and absences for school sponsored activities.  For excused absences, not school related, students have one (1) day for each day absent to make up work missed.  Any exception to this will be made by the principal.  Students who are absent for school related business know about the expected day of absence ahead of time and must make arrangements with their teachers for their assignments and tests prior to the absence.  Class assignments must be made up the day following the absence.  Tests will be made up at the discretion of the teacher.  It is a teacher's responsibility to set a time for make-up tests.  It is the student's responsibility to take the test at that time unless there are unavoidable conflicts and those must be worked out with the teacher.

STUDENT ATTENDANCE - ABSENCES FOR EXTRA-CURRICULAR ACTIVITIES:  A student can miss no more than ten (10) days a year, five per term course, from a class to participate in extra-curricular activities.  Teacher sponsors of extra-curricular activities requiring students to be absent from classes are responsible for notifying teachers and the principal's office.  An alphabetical listing of students (form found in campus canvas course), including grade level and I.D. number, must be sent to the principal, the attendance office, and to the appropriate teachers no later than three (3) days prior to an event.  All school related activities that require students to miss classes must be approved by the building principal no less than five school days prior to the activity. Failure to obtain approval and failure to notify appropriate school personnel by the specified times will void participation in the activity.  Subject area teachers must keep an accurate record of student absences for extra-curricular activities.  If a student accumulates ten absences due to these activities, the teacher must inform the student, the attendance office, and the building principal.

Students will have three (3) days in which to clear the unexcused absence to an excused one.  If the attendance office has not updated attendance within this three-day period, the absence remains unexcused for the duration of the term in which the absence occurred. Exceptions can be made by admin, at which time the attendance office will make the update.

Students are allowed 5 absences within a year that can be excused by a parent. Unexcused absences count towards the 5 allowed. Starting at the 6th absence (not counting those excused with a doctor’s note), they can no longer be excused with a parent note. Doctor’s notes excuse any absence. If a student attends 1 minute of class during the school day and provides a doctor’s note dated that day, any time missed will be changed to present.

Being on time for work, dates, appointments, meetings, etc., is a good habit to develop.  Begin developing this habit by being on time to school/classes.  Tardiness disrupts the learning environment in the classroom.  Therefore, tardies to class are not permitted.  Students should be inside their classroom before the tardy bell rings each period.
TARDY POLICY

items: Training days will be given at the start of each semester -- excused tardies.
For every tardy, a student must scan the QR code for documentation purposes.

FIELD TRIPS AND SCHOOL RELATED ABSENCES FOR STUDENTS....GUIDELINES

items: Complete a field trip form with the names of students listed and get approval signatures from the principal.
Provide a list of the students (email) to be absent to the remaining teachers and THE ATTENDANCE OFFICE seven (10) days prior to the absence.
It is the teacher’s responsibility NOT the student’s to notify the other teachers of the absence.  In fact, please do not leave it up to the student to notify his/her other teachers of the absence.
A LISD PERMISSION FORM must also be completed by the student and their parent/guardian with proper signatures and this must be placed on file in the attendance office. (board policy)
Field Trips will NOT be  approved during assigned dead weeks.
DEAD WEEK DATES:
(NO FIELD TRIPS ARE ALLOWED DURING DEAD WEEKS, including STAAR weeks
and the last two weeks of school. SEE DATES ABOVE under 'Dead Week')

blocks.html: *NOTE All teachers and staff...Do not drive students in your personal vehicle to or from school related events or at any other time for that matter.

items: Always use a school vehicle when transporting students to school related events/activities.

Any teacher or adult that drives students must have completed a driver’s license check and be approved by the district.  All the above is for liability purposes and the protection of yourself and our students.

blocks.html: DISCIPLINE WITH DIGNITY: Maintaining an orderly and controlled (safe and orderly) school is considered to be the first priority of the school administrator.  Maintaining an orderly and controlled (safe and orderly) classroom is considered to be the first responsibility of the classroom teacher.  Copies of teachers’ individual classroom rules and expectations are to be given to students at the first of the term both fall and spring treatment agreements are to be created with their students at the beginning of the term for both Fall and Spring semesters. They should be easily visible and posted in the classroom at all times.  Additional information will be given about discipline and the Discipline Management System.  Teachers are required to follow the procedures specified by administration in referring students for discipline.  Discipline Referral Forms can be found in the Faculty Center. *Tweak with specific details at a later date.

STRATEGIES FOR EFFECTIVE DISCIPLINE: Be proactive, not reactive.  Visibility in the hallways and bathrooms to stop problems before they start is the best control measure we have.  Stand at your doors or move around in the halls during passing periods, making sure you address each possible infraction that could occur.
The following statements are hints to help your team and individual discipline plans be successful:

items: Always strive to convey to the student that it is not a personal affront, but an infraction of the rules being addressed when correcting student behavior.
Take the time to explain to students that rules and regulations exist for everyone’s best interest. Point out that “real life” places the same demands on us as educators do on the students.
If a student needs to be removed from the classroom, call Ms. Werstak in the front office (4500) and the admin will assist at your location.
Never, except if necessary to protect yourself and others, put your hands on a student during a time of confrontation.

Name-calling, sarcasm, and personal humiliation of the student should never be used as a means of discipline or behavior correction.  Never say anything to a student that you would not say to him/her in front of his/her parents. Sarcasm is an unfair tactic, and usually brings an equally sarcastic reply.

blocks.html: There are several disciplinary actions the administration may utilize as consequences for student misbehavior.  Under normal circumstances a hierarchy of discipline management techniques are applied in an attempt to alter student conduct.  Teachers are to make contact with parents regarding student's behavior.  A list of disciplinary actions that may be used by the administrator follows:
Students may be spoken with by the assistant principal and/or the counselor.
School detentions are before and after school from 8:20 to 8:45 a.m. and 4:20 to 5:15 p.m. and will be assigned by the administration.

Positive Learning Environment (formerly ISS) removes students from their regular school setting while keeping them in the building.  Teachers are required to send assignments to the PLE teacher before the start of the student's first day in PLE.  No academic penalty is to be applied.  The PLE teacher  will record attendance and teachers will be notified of absences.
A student may be suspended from school for no more than 3 days.  Teachers will furnish assignments if requested.  The student's absence will be considered an excused absence.

Students may be placed in DAEP for various offenses. Some assignments will need to be provided by the home campus and others will be provided by DAEP staff. Home campus is responsible for inputting grades weekly. Grades will be sent to home campus by DAEP.  There is no academic penalty.
A student may be expelled after due process has been followed.  The length of expulsion will be determined on an individual basis.
STUDENT REMOVAL: If a student needs to be removed from the classroom, call Ms. Werstak in the front office (4500) and the admin will assist at your location.
LHSK BEHAVIOR MANAGEMENT PLAN
